techniques.evidence_quote: The attacker compromised the root IAM user access key and used it to enumerate the environment and spin up ten (10) c5.4xlarge EC2s to mine Monero.
For reference, below are the MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise and Cloud Tactics observed during Expel's response: Execution | Scripting
The CoinMiner was likely installed via SSH remote access (as a part of the bot).
For reference, below are the MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise and Cloud Tactics observed: Persistence | Valid Accounts, Redundant Access

The root access key was used to access the AWS environment from a cloud hosting environment using the AWS Golang SDK. It was then used to create SSH keys, spin up EC2 instances via the RunInstances API call and created new security groups likely to allow inbound access from the Internet.
The root AWS access key was potentially compromised.
The attacker compromised the root IAM user access key and used it to enumerate the environment and spin up ten (10) c5.4xlarge EC2s.

The CoinMiner was likely installed via SSH remote access (as a part of the bot). We didn't have local EC2 visibility to confirm, but an ingress rule was created in the bot automation to allow SSH from the Internet.
AWS GuardDuty alerts for an EC2 instance communicating with a cryptocurrency server (monerohash[.]com on TCP port 7777).
spin up ten (10) c5.4xlarge EC2s to mine Monero.

attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained and leveraged a compromised root IAM user access key to authenticate to the AWS environment from a cloud hosting provider IP using the AWS Golang SDK.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker compromised the root IAM user access key and used it to enumerate the environment.

attack_chain.description: Attacker enumerated the AWS environment using the root access key, issuing API calls (non-Get/List/Describe) to understand the infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The root access key was used to access the AWS environment... Ruxie pulled up API calls made by the principal (interesting in this context is mostly anything that isn't Get*, List*, Describe* and Head*).

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the root access key to generate SSH key pairs for EC2 instances via the ImportKeyPair API from a cloud hosting provider IP, establishing a mechanism for SSH access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: EC2 SSH access keys were generated for the root access key from a suspicious source IP address using the AWS Golang SDK... This was the first time we saw an attacker bring their own SSH key pairs that were uniquely named.

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: Attacker created new EC2 security groups to allow inbound SSH access from the Internet, modifying network controls to enable subsequent lateral access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: created new security groups likely to allow inbound access from the Internet... an ingress rule was created in the bot automation to allow SSH from the Internet.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Attacker used the root access key to spin up ten c5.4xlarge EC2 instances via the RunInstances API call to serve as cryptomining workers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: spin up ten (10) c5.4xlarge EC2s to mine Monero... spin up EC2 instances via the RunInstances API call.

attack_chain.description: Attacker SSHed into the newly spun-up EC2 instances using the imported SSH key pairs to install CoinMiner malware.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The CoinMiner was likely installed via SSH remote access (as a part of the bot). We didn't have local EC2 visibility to confirm, but an ingress rule was created in the bot automation to allow SSH from the Internet.

attack_chain.description: Attacker executed scripting/automation (AWS Golang SDK bot) to orchestrate the full attack chain, including key import, instance creation, security group modification, and miner installation.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This was also the first time we'd observed a bot written in the AWS Golang software development kit (SDK).

attack_chain.description: Attacker deployed Monero cryptomining software on the ten EC2 instances, which communicated with the monerohash[.]com mining pool over TCP port 7777.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: AWS GuardDuty alerts for an EC2 instance communicating with a cryptocurrency server (monerohash[.]com on TCP port 7777).

attack_chain.description: Miner C2 communications used TCP port 7777 (non-standard) to connect to the monerohash[.]com mining pool, evading standard port-based detection.

initial_access_vector: Root IAM user access key compromised (exact compromise vector unknown; key was likely scraped and passed to an automated bot)
persistence_mechanism: Attacker imported uniquely-named SSH key pairs via the EC2 ImportKeyPair API and created permissive security groups allowing inbound SSH from the Internet, enabling persistent access to newly provisioned EC2 instances
privilege_escalation: No privilege escalation required — attacker entered with root IAM access key, the highest-privilege credential in the AWS account
